text:
The study addresses the questions of what knowledge and skills do university presidents say are essential to contemporary executive leadership in higher education. Interviews were conducted with 13 of the 25 sitting presidents in state universities who hold doctoral degrees in higher education administration. Using qualitative procedures, the study examines the responsibilities of a presidency that has become a much more complex, ambiguous, powerful and administrative office.

The research identified the essential knowledge that presidents are required to have in three major categories: (1) core knowledge, consisting of the history of higher education and finance; (2) cultural knowledge, which comprises contextual and image-building skills; and (3) advanced cognitive skills. In terms of competencies, presidents were required to possess personal competencies including vision and humility, as well as the management competencies of evaluation, recruitment, fundraising, and strategic planning, in addition to communication competencies with a multifaceted character. Overall, their performance entailed not only academic leadership but also administrative skills, reminiscent of a business chief executive officer that is appropriate to the complex demands and constituencies in today's institutions. The implications of the study for enhancing leadership preparation programs for doctoral students and for trustees when choosing a president and determining the appropriate preparation are highlighted.
